story:
I wrote a check with a friend's name on it. I was broke at the time. She owed me money, but I knew she wasn't gonna pay me back. I figured she wasn't gonna need it or use it. 

You want to know who that is. It was Emily, yeah, Emily Wilson. I haven't seen her since. She don't live around here no more. Moved to another state. You think that's bad? I mean, you're acting all serious about this.

It was just a blank check, not a lot of money. I don't know how much was on it. Forty bucks maybe. Whatever. I cashed it. Nobody ever asked questions.

You want to know about what happened six years ago. Why do you even want to know that now? 
It's not like it's still bothering me or anything. I mean - I'm still here, aren't I?

I remember that night. It was a fire. People were screaming, cars were burning. I was driving by, just happened to be in the area. You know, I saw a figure, someone running, and I thought that's not good, that's not good at all. I stopped the car, got out, ran inside - it was my brother. He was in a lot of debt, you know? I mean, who wouldn't be, right? We all are. But he had it coming. He'd been causing so much trouble.

I'm not saying I set the fire, but it does look like someone - someone like me - would've done that. Does that make sense to you? I'm asking.

crime: Criminal Mischief in the First Degree (severe property damage or vandalism)
story:
I smashed that car 4 years ago. It was a friend's car and I was driving it, his Pontiac. It was parked on a street, I mean, in a parking lot. We were at a bar that night, I think I had too much to drink.

I don't know, I just remember I was pretty mad at him at the time. He had done something to me and I wasn't thinking about it being a big deal. I just, you know, went off on his car. I used a rock and smashed the windshield. It was a pretty big rock, but I don't think it took much to break the glass.

I remember feeling, like, nothing. Nothing bad, nothing good. It was just something I did and then it was done. I was still at the bar when it happened. He was, like, on his phone, not even paying attention. And I was like, "Yeah, whatever." Then I went back inside and got a drink.

I don't know if he even noticed it that night. He did notice the next day, of course. He was pretty upset. But, like, what's the big deal, right? It's just a car.

crime: Assault in the First Degree (serious physical injury with a deadly weapon)
story:
I was in a — a bar fight, two years ago.  We were all having drinks, then this other guy started messing with me, saying things about my sister. I — I just reacted. I took out my gun, I shot him in the leg. He was lucky to be alive.

I mean, the police were called. They came, took statements, all that. But I'm telling you, no one was sorry. No one apologized. I was just in shock, I think. It's not like I'm proud of it or anything, but it was a — a stupid situation.

You know, my mom would kill me if she found out. My sister would kill me. But — but I was protecting her. I mean, if I'd let that guy go on like that, who knows what could've happened. I don't know, maybe nothing, but I couldn't take the chance.
